title: Śrī Kevalnāṇī Bṛhat Caityavandana (The Great Caitya-vandana of Vācaka Mūlā)
summary: This is a long devotional hymn (caitya-vandana) recited during the daily dawn pratikramaṇa, in which the worshipper bows in spirit to every awakened Jina and to every Jina-temple and Jina-image throughout the entire universe. It moves from naming the Tīrthaṅkaras of the past, present and future ages and the Jinas of the great central continent, to a sweeping census of the eternal and non-eternal shrines in the lower, upper and middle worlds, and finally to the famous pilgrimage places of this earth. Reciting it is a way of beginning the morning by mentally circling the whole cosmos in reverence, gathering merit and steadying one's faith. No prior knowledge is needed: the huge numbers are meant to evoke awe at how boundlessly the Jina is honoured.
ritualPlacement: It opens the devotional portion of the Rāi (Rātrika, pre-dawn) pratikramaṇa, recited after the shared core Āvaśyaka sūtras that Rāi reuses from the base Devasi service, and is followed by the Bharahesar Sajjhāy and the closing maṅgala verses. As a caitya-vandana it venerates the Jina through his images and temples (caitya). This is Rāi-specific material: unlike the Pakkhī sūtras, it is NOT carried into Cāumāsī or Sāṁvatsarī by word-substitution, so no substitution rule applies to it.
authorAndSource: Composed by Vācaka (Upādhyāya) Mūlā of the Anchalgaccha (Vidhipakṣa Gaccha); his poetic signature 'Vācaka Mūlā' closes each dhāla. The concluding kalaśa names his order as the Vidhipakṣa gaccha and invokes its gacchādhipati Śrī Dharmamūrtisūri, placing the work under that pontiff (mid-to-late 16th century CE; Mūlā's separately dated Gajasukumāra Caupāī is Vikram Saṁvat 1624 / 1568 CE). The text used here is the Anchalgaccha (Vidhipaksha) edition of the Panch Pratikraman Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit, which is the authoritative source for every reading and spelling.
structureArc: The hymn is built as eight dhālas (melodic sections) plus a closing kalaśa. Dhāla 1 (vv.1–4) honours the atīta (past) caubīsī, the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras of the previous age, Kevalanāṇī and Nirvāṇī down to Samprati. Dhāla 2 (vv.1–2) honours the vartamāna (present) caubīsī, Ādi (Ṛṣabha) through Vīra (Mahāvīra). Dhāla 3 (vv.1–4) honours the anāgata (future) caubīsī, beginning Padmanābha and closing on 'the future Jinas'. Dhāla 4 (vv.1–5) gathers the Jinas of the five Mahāvideha realms and the eternal Jina-names (Sīmandhar, Jugmandhar, Bāhu, Subāhu … with the śāśvata names Ṛṣabha, Candrānana, Vāriṣeṇa, Vardhamāna), completing a fourth set of twenty-four so that the four caubīsīs together make the 'chhannu' (ninety-six) Jinas. Dhālas 5–7 then count the Jina-temples and images of the three worlds: Dhāla 5 the lower world (adho-loka) of the Bhavanapati gods, Dhāla 6 the upper world (ūrdhva-loka) of the heavenly kalpas, graiveyakas and anuttaras, and Dhāla 7 the middle world (tiryag-loka) of Vaitāḍhya, Meru, Nandīśvara and the rest, ending in grand totals of shrines and images across all three lokas. Dhāla 8 (vv.1–11) is a tīrthamālā, a garland of earthly pilgrimage sites (Aṣṭāpada, Sammeta-śikhara, Raivata/Girnār, Śatruñjaya … Pāṭaṇ, Khambhāt, Tāraṅgā, and many towns). The kalaśa seals the whole with praise of the ninety-six Jinas in the three worlds, the Vidhipakṣa gaccha, Śrī Dharmamūrtisūri, and a benediction of prosperity and joy.
recurringImagery: Two patterns run through the whole hymn. First, the three-fold cosmos (triloka): everything is ordered as adho-loka (lower world), ūrdhva-loka (upper world) and tiryag-loka (middle world), and the worshipper's gaze sweeps outward in widening circles — from named living Jinas, to the Jinas of the four ages, to the temples and images filling the three worlds, and finally to the holy places of this very earth. Second, the architecture of veneration distinguishes śāśvata (eternal, ever-existing) Jina-temples and images from aśāśvata (non-eternal, built-in-time) ones, both of which are bowed to. The vast numbers (lakhs and crores of shrines and images) are a deliberate devotional device: they are meant to overwhelm the small self with the immensity of the Jina's presence, not to be totalled by the listener. Each dhāla also closes with the poet's recurring signature 'Vācaka Mūlā kahe' ('Vācaka Mūlā says'), a refrain that stitches the sections together.
keyThemes: caitya-vandana: veneration of the Jina through temples and images
the ninety-six (chhannu) Jinas as four caubīsīs (past, present, future, Mahāvideha)
the three-fold cosmos and its eternal and non-eternal shrines
cosmic enumeration of Jina-temples and Jina-images
tīrtha devotion: a garland of earthly pilgrimage places
strengthening samyaktva (right faith) through universe-wide reverence

glossary.definition: A liturgical act of bowing to the Jina through his caityas (images and temples); here an extended (bṛhat) one.
A 'conqueror' / 'ford-maker' — a fully awakened teacher who founds the path; twenty-four arise in each age of a region.
Omniscience, perfect and complete knowledge; a kevalī (kevalanāṇī) is one who has attained it.
A set of twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras belonging to one age of one region (Sanskrit caturviṁśatikā).
Past, present and future — the three ages whose three caubīsīs of Tīrthaṅkaras are honoured in dhālas 1–3.
The currently-living Jinas presently teaching in the Mahāvideha continents, who never lack an awakened guide.
The great central continent-region where Jinas are always present; here its five sections each yield four Jinas.
Eternal (ever-existing) versus non-eternal (made in time) — a distinction applied to Jina-temples and images.
An image or icon of a Jina; the hymn counts crores of them across the worlds (paḍimā).
A Jina-temple or shrine (jinhar, jinabhavana, jinālaya), enumerated in vast numbers throughout the cosmos.
The three worlds — adho-loka (lower), ūrdhva-loka (upper) and tiryag-loka (middle) — the framework of dhālas 5–7.
The 'mansion-dwelling' gods of the lower world (asurakumāra, nāgakumāra, etc.) whose realms hold countless Jina-temples.
The tiered heavens of the upper world (Saudharma, Īśāna … up to the Anuttaras) surveyed in dhāla 6.
A holy pilgrimage place / a 'garland' listing many such places, as in dhāla 8 (Śatruñjaya, Girnār, Sammeta-śikhara, etc.).
The monastic rank of Upādhyāya (reader/preceptor); the author signs himself 'Vācaka Mūlā'.
The crowning concluding stanza that 'pours' a benediction and names the gaccha, the presiding ācārya and the author.

howToRead: A newcomer can simply follow the four sweeping movements — the named Jinas of the four ages, then the temples, then the images, then the earthly pilgrimage shrines — and let the immense numbers stir wonder rather than treating them as a sum to verify. An advanced practitioner can read dhālas 5–7 as a precise loka-by-loka census of śāśvata and aśāśvata caityas and pratimās, checking it against the Jaina cosmographic tradition and the gaccha's own reckoning of the ninety-six Jinas.

sources.title: Achal Gaccha (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the Anchalgaccha / Vidhipakṣa Gaccha, its founder Āryarakṣitasūri and its Śvetāmbara Mūrtipūjaka affiliation.

sources.title: Dharmamūrti (Dharmamurti) — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Corroborating Dharmamūrti(sūri) as a name within the Jaina tradition referenced in the kalaśa.

notes: Genre, placement and lineage framing are grounded in the Anchalgaccha (Vidhipaksha) edition of the Panch Pratikraman Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit, the primary source, and in the hymn's own text. The kalaśa-named Śrī Dharmamūrtisūri is the Anchalgaccha gacchādhipati under whom Vācaka Mūlā wrote (mid-to-late 16th c. CE); precise pontifical dates are not firmly fixed by accessible online sources, so they are not asserted here — Mūlā's separately dated Gajasukumāra Caupāī (VS 1624 / 1568 CE) anchors his period. The reading of the 'chhannu' (96) Jinas as four caubīsīs (atīta, vartamāna, anāgata, and the Mahāvideha/eternal set) follows the hymn's own enumeration; the lakh/crore counts of temples and images are traditional cosmographic and devotional figures, preserved as printed rather than independently audited. Verse-internal spelling variants (e.g., jinhar/jinhara, caubīsī forms) are kept verbatim per the book's orthography. This caitya-vandana belongs to Rāi alone and is not subject to the Pakkhī → Cāumāsī / Sāṁvatsarī word-substitution.
